bibliographic_data.title: ScanSSD: Scanning Single Shot Detector for Mathematical Formulas in PDF Document Images
bibliographic_data.abstract: We introduce the Scanning Single Shot Detector (ScanSSD) for locating math formulas offset from text and embedded in textlines. ScanSSD uses only visual features for detection: no formatting or typesetting information such as layout, font, or character labels are employed. Given a 600 dpi document page image, a Single Shot Detector (SSD) locates formulas at multiple scales using sliding windows, after which candidate detections are pooled to obtain page-level results. For our experiments we use the TFD-ICDAR2019v2 dataset, a modification of the GTDB scanned math article collection. ScanSSD detects characters in formulas with high accuracy, obtaining a 0.926 f-score, and detects formulas with high recall overall. Detection errors are largely minor, such as splitting formulas at large whitespace gaps (e.g., for variable constraints) and merging formulas on adjacent textlines. Formula detection f-scores of 0.796 (IOU ≥0.5) and 0.733 (IOU ≥0.75) are obtained. Our data, evaluation tools, and code are publicly available.

core_content.novelty_claim: Introduces ScanSSD, a visual feature-based detector for mathematical formulas in both born-digital and scanned PDF documents.
core_content.key_methodology: Uses a Single Shot Detector (SSD) to locate formulas at multiple scales using sliding windows on 600 dpi document page images. Page-level formula detections are obtained by pooling SSD region detections through voting and thresholding.
outcomes: Detects characters in formulas with high accuracy, achieving an f-score of 0.926.
Detects formula regions accurately enough to be used as a baseline for indexing mathematical formulas.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper introduces ScanSSD, a Single Shot Detector (SSD) that uses only visual features to detect mathematical formulas in PDF documents. The method is applied on 600 dpi document images and locates formulas at multiple scales using sliding windows. After detection, candidate regions are pooled to obtain page-level results. The system achieves high accuracy in detecting characters with an f-score of 0.926 and overall formula detection recall. Minor errors include splitting formulas due to large whitespace gaps and merging formulas on adjacent textlines. The ScanSSD code is publicly available.
